title: Branson Bransonic Series Ultrasonic Cleaner - Unit Will Not Start
description: Troubleshooting a Bransonic ultrasonic cleaner that will not begin a cycle due to power, lid, settings, fluid level, or control issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is not being used for active instrument processing and that staff have an alternate cleaner available if needed. Do not reach into the tank while the unit is energized, and do not operate the cleaner empty. Expected outcome: The unit can be evaluated safely without risk of electrical shock, burns, or improper instrument reprocessing.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Ask staff what happens when they try to start the unit. Check whether the unit has: No display or no lights; Display present but no cycle start; Button response but no ultrasonic activity; Timer set but unit does not run; Intermittent starting. Expected outcome: The failure mode is confirmed before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Check the Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the power cord is fully seated at the unit and plugged into a known-good outlet. Test the outlet with another device or outlet tester if available. Expected outcome: The unit receives proper facility power. If the unit powers on after correcting the outlet or plug connection, return it to service after confirming normal operation and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Check the power cord for cuts, crushed areas, loose strain relief, bent plug blades, burn marks, or signs of overheating. Expected outcome: The cord and plug are intact and safe to use. If damage is found, remove the unit from service. Do not continue testing with a damaged cord.

steps.title: Confirm the Power Switch or Control Panel Is On
steps.instructions: Verify the main power switch, if equipped, is in the ON position. On digital models, confirm the display illuminates and the control panel responds. On analog models, confirm the timer and heat controls move normally. Expected outcome: The unit shows normal standby behavior.

steps.title: Check Timer or Cycle Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm a valid cleaning time has been selected. Some ultrasonic cleaners will not start if the timer is set to zero, paused, or not fully engaged. Expected outcome: A valid cycle time is selected and the unit is ready to run. If the unit starts after correcting the timer setting, verify normal ultrasonic activity and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Tank Fluid Level
steps.instructions: Confirm the tank is filled to the proper operating level with approved cleaning solution or water-based solution per facility process. Do not test ultrasonic operation with an empty or underfilled tank. Expected outcome: The tank has enough fluid for safe ultrasonic operation. If the unit starts after correcting the fluid level, document the finding and return it to service if performance is normal.

steps.title: Check for Lid, Basket, or Accessory Interference
steps.instructions: Inspect the lid, basket, tray, and instrument load for improper placement. Make sure nothing is pressing against the control panel, preventing lid closure where applicable, or binding against the tank. Expected outcome: Accessories are correctly installed and not interfering with startup.

steps.title: Inspect for Visible Liquid Intrusion or Spillage
steps.instructions: Look around the control panel, front panel, power switch, rear connections, and underside for signs of spilled fluid, corrosion, residue, or leakage. Expected outcome: No evidence of fluid entry or external leakage is present. If fluid intrusion is suspected, remove the unit from service. Do not continue applying power.

steps.title: Check for Overheating or Abnormal Odor
steps.instructions: Before attempting repeated starts, check for unusual heat, burning smell, buzzing, smoke, or discoloration. Expected outcome: The unit shows no signs of electrical overheating. If overheating, smoke, or burning odor is present, unplug the unit, label it Out of Service, and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Unit
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, unplug it, wait at least one minute, then reconnect it to a verified outlet. Attempt to start a normal short cycle with the tank properly filled. Expected outcome: The unit resets and starts normally. If the unit starts and runs normally, document the reset and verify operation before returning it to service.

steps.title: Test Basic Ultrasonic Operation
steps.instructions: If the unit starts but there is no cleaning action, listen for normal ultrasonic activity and observe for proper cavitation behavior using facility-approved testing methods. Expected outcome: The unit starts and produces normal ultrasonic action. If the controls start but no ultrasonic activity occurs, treat this as a performance failure rather than a simple start failure.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Repeated Start Attempts
steps.instructions: If the unit still will not start after power, outlet, cord, timer, fluid level, accessories, and obvious external issues are checked, stop troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Further use is avoided until the unit is evaluated.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Branson Bransonic ultrasonic cleaner would not start when a cleaning cycle was selected.
CCR.Cause: Verified outlet power and cord condition; unit displayed power but would not begin a cycle after timer and fluid level were confirmed.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for bench evaluation for suspected internal control or power-start circuit failure.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested or alternate outlet used
Lid, basket, and accessory placement checked
Any abnormal odor, heat, buzzing, or smoke
Whether the unit is analog or digital control
Whether the issue is intermittent or constant

title: Branson Bransonic Series Ultrasonic Cleaner - Unit Operates But Solution Does Not Heat
description: Troubleshooting a Bransonic ultrasonic cleaner that powers on and runs sonics, but the bath solution does not heat properly.

steps.title: Ensure Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is not being used for active instrument reprocessing before troubleshooting. Allow the tank and solution to cool before handling, draining, or moving the cleaner. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is performed without burn risk, splash exposure, or interruption to clinical workflow.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Heating Issue
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed: Unit powers on; Timer or cycle runs; Sonics appear to operate; Solution remains cold or only slightly warm; Heat indicator does or does not illuminate; Issue occurs every use or intermittently. Expected outcome: The complaint is confirmed as a heating problem, not a general power or cycle-start issue.

steps.title: Check That the Heat Function Is Actually Enabled
steps.instructions: Verify the temperature control or heat setting is turned on and set above the current solution temperature. Some ultrasonic cleaners can operate sonics without heating if heat is off or set too low. Expected outcome: Heat is selected and the set temperature is high enough to call for heating. If the heat setting was off or set too low, correct the setting, allow the unit time to warm, verify heating, document the finding, and stop.

steps.title: Allow Adequate Warm-Up Time
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit was given enough time to heat the solution before staff reported the failure. Ultrasonic baths do not heat instantly, especially when filled with cold water or a high solution volume. Expected outcome: The solution temperature should gradually rise over time when heat is enabled. If the unit heats normally after adequate warm-up time, return it to service and document user education.

steps.title: Check Fill Level
steps.instructions: Inspect the tank fill level. The solution should be within the manufacturer’s normal operating range and not below the minimum fill line. Expected outcome: The tank contains enough solution for safe heating and ultrasonic operation. If the tank is underfilled, correct the fill level, retest heating, and stop if the issue is resolved.

steps.title: Check for Excessive Solution Volume or Cold Starting Conditions
steps.instructions: Determine whether staff filled the tank with very cold water, overfilled the tank, or used a large volume of solution immediately before use. Expected outcome: Heating expectations are realistic based on starting temperature and fill volume. If the cleaner heats slowly but reaches temperature, document normal operation and educate staff.

steps.title: Verify Power Source
steps.instructions: Check that the ultrasonic cleaner is plugged directly into a known-good hospital-grade outlet, unless facility policy allows another approved configuration. Avoid power strips, loose outlet connections, damaged cords, or shared circuits when evaluating heating complaints. Expected outcome: The unit receives stable power. If the outlet is defective or unstable, move the unit to a verified outlet, retest, document the outlet issue, and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Look for: Bent plug blades; Damaged insulation; Loose strain relief; Burn marks; Heat damage; Intermittent power when the cord is gently repositioned. Expected outcome: Cord and plug are intact with no visible damage. If cord damage is found, remove the unit from service and send it for repair or cord replacement evaluation.

steps.title: Inspect the Tank and Exterior Condition
steps.instructions: Check for signs that may indicate unsafe operation: Leaking tank; Corrosion; Cracked housing; Liquid under the unit; Unusual odor; Discoloration from overheating; Excessive external heat. Expected outcome: The unit is physically safe to operate. If leakage, burning smell, or abnormal heat is present, unplug the unit, label it Out of Service, and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Confirm the Lid and Normal Use Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify staff are using the lid if required by department practice. An open tank can lose heat faster and may appear to heat poorly, especially in cooler rooms. Expected outcome: The unit is tested under normal operating conditions. If heating improves with proper lid use and normal setup, document the finding and return the unit to service.

steps.title: Check for Heater Indicator Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe whether the heat indicator light or display icon activates when heat is enabled. Expected outcome: The unit shows that it is calling for heat. If the heater indicator does not activate despite proper settings, suspect a control, thermostat, sensor, or heater circuit issue.

steps.title: Measure Solution Temperature Externally
steps.instructions: Use an appropriate external thermometer to compare actual bath temperature to the expected heating behavior. Do not rely only on touch. Expected outcome: Temperature should rise over time when heat is enabled. If the temperature rises normally, the unit may be functioning correctly and staff expectations or warm-up time may be the issue.

steps.title: Run a Controlled Bench Test
steps.instructions: With the unit off patient-care workflow, fill to the proper level with room-temperature water or approved test solution. Turn heat on, set the temperature appropriately, and monitor temperature rise over a reasonable interval. Expected outcome: The bath temperature increases steadily. If the unit operates but the solution temperature does not rise at all, external causes have likely been ruled out.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Heating
steps.instructions: If heating works sometimes but not consistently, gently observe for changes related to: Cord movement; Control setting movement; Display changes; Heat indicator flickering; Unit vibration during sonics. Expected outcome: Heating remains stable during operation. If heating is intermittent, remove the device from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Do Not Perform Internal Heater Repair During Field Troubleshooting
steps.instructions: Do not open the unit for board-level diagnosis, heater replacement, thermostat replacement, or internal wiring repair during basic troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Clinical Engineering stops once external causes and safe operational checks are complete.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Branson Bransonic ultrasonic cleaner powered on and completed cycles, but the solution did not heat.
CCR.Cause: Verified proper outlet power, fill level, and heat setting; bath temperature failed to rise during controlled bench testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for repair evaluation for suspected internal heater or temperature-control failure.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual odor, heat, leakage, or sound

title: Branson Bransonic Series Ultrasonic Cleaner - Weak or Absent Ultrasonic Cleaning Action
description: Troubleshooting poor cavitation, weak cleaning, absent ultrasonic activity, improper loading, solution issues, or failed ultrasonic output.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the ultrasonic cleaner is not being used for active instrument processing while troubleshooting. Remove instruments from the tank before testing, and follow facility infection control procedures when handling contaminated items or cleaning solution. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is performed safely without interrupting instrument reprocessing or exposing staff to contaminated solution.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Cleaning Problem
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed: Instruments still visibly soiled after a cycle; No audible ultrasonic activity; Weak or uneven cleaning; Foil test or cavitation test failure; Unit runs but does not appear to clean; Problem began after solution change, relocation, or heavy loading. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as weak or absent ultrasonic cleaning action rather than a timer, heater, drain, or user-process issue.

steps.title: Check That the Unit Powers On and Starts a Cycle
steps.instructions: Verify the Bransonic unit powers on normally and that the timer or cycle control starts as expected. Observe whether the display, indicator light, or cycle status behaves normally during operation. Expected outcome: The unit starts a cycle and appears to operate. If the unit does not power on or cannot start a cycle, troubleshoot the power or control issue first and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Tank Has the Correct Solution Level
steps.instructions: Inspect the tank and verify the liquid level is within the marked operating range. Do not operate the ultrasonic cleaner with low solution level or an empty tank. Expected outcome: The tank is filled to the proper level. Why it matters: Low solution level can reduce ultrasonic energy transfer and may damage the unit.

steps.title: Verify Proper Cleaning Solution Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Confirm staff are using an approved ultrasonic cleaning solution at the correct dilution. Check for plain water use, incorrect detergent, excessive concentration, expired solution, or solution that is visibly dirty. Expected outcome: The tank contains fresh, properly mixed ultrasonic cleaning solution. If replacing the solution restores cleaning performance, document the cause and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Solution Has Been Degassed
steps.instructions: If the solution was recently changed, run the unit empty with only solution in the tank for several minutes to allow trapped air to clear. Expected outcome: Ultrasonic action improves after degassing. Why it matters: Fresh solution can hold air bubbles that reduce cavitation and make cleaning appear weak.

steps.title: Inspect Loading and Basket Use
steps.instructions: Verify instruments are placed in the basket and are not resting directly on the tank bottom. Check for overloaded baskets, stacked instruments, closed hinges, heavy contact between items, or instruments blocking ultrasonic energy. Expected outcome: Instruments are loosely arranged, fully submerged, open at hinges, and not touching the tank floor. If correcting the load restores cleaning performance, document the user-process cause and stop.

steps.title: Check for Items That Absorb or Block Ultrasonic Energy
steps.instructions: Look for soft materials, absorbent items, silicone mats, towels, plastics, or incorrect accessories placed in the tank. Expected outcome: Only appropriate instruments and approved accessories are present. Why it matters: Incorrect items can absorb ultrasonic energy and reduce effective cleaning.

steps.title: Listen for Normal Ultrasonic Activity
steps.instructions: Start a cycle with the tank filled correctly and listen for the normal ultrasonic buzzing or hissing sound. Avoid placing hands in the operating tank. Expected outcome: A steady ultrasonic sound is heard during the active cycle. If the timer runs but there is no ultrasonic sound, ultrasonic output failure is more likely after external causes are ruled out.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Cavitation Check if Facility Procedure Allows
steps.instructions: Use the facility-approved cavitation test method, such as a foil test or commercial ultrasonic activity indicator. Place the test material according to facility procedure and run a normal cycle. Expected outcome: The test shows consistent cavitation across the tank. If the test passes, the device is producing ultrasonic activity and the issue may be related to solution, loading, exposure time, or instrument preparation.

steps.title: Check for Uneven Cavitation Across the Tank
steps.instructions: If testing shows activity in some areas but not others, repeat the test with fresh solution and a proper solution level. Expected outcome: Cavitation should be reasonably consistent across the tank. If dead zones remain after fresh solution and proper setup, internal transducer or generator issues may be present.

steps.title: Inspect the Tank for Visible Damage
steps.instructions: With the unit powered off and unplugged, inspect the tank for pitting, cracks, residue buildup, scale, discoloration, or signs of leakage. Expected outcome: The tank is clean, intact, and free of damage that could affect ultrasonic performance. If the tank is damaged or leaking, remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Verify Cycle Time and Temperature Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm staff are using an appropriate cycle time for the soil load and instrument type. If the unit has heat, verify the selected temperature is appropriate and that staff are not expecting heat alone to perform ultrasonic cleaning. Expected outcome: Cycle settings match facility reprocessing procedure.

steps.title: Check for Unusual Heat, Smell, or Noise
steps.instructions: During a safe test cycle, observe for abnormal buzzing, intermittent operation, burning smell, excessive heat, or unusual vibration. Expected outcome: The unit operates consistently without abnormal odor, overheating, or erratic sound. If unusual heat, odor, or intermittent ultrasonic operation is present, stop testing and remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Retest After Correcting External Conditions
steps.instructions: After confirming fresh solution, correct fill level, degassing, proper loading, and correct cycle settings, repeat the cavitation check. Expected outcome: Ultrasonic cleaning action returns to normal. If performance is restored, return the device to service after cleaning, inspection, and documentation.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Branson Bransonic ultrasonic cleaner was running but instruments remained visibly soiled after the cycle.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the tank solution was freshly changed but had not been degassed, resulting in weak cavitation during testing.
CCR.Resolution: Degassed the solution, verified correct fill level and loading, repeated cavitation test successfully, and returned the ultrasonic cleaner to service.

helpfulDetails: Instruments not resting on tank bottom
Load size and instrument placement verified
Unusual noise, heat, or odor noted

title: Branson Bransonic Series Ultrasonic Cleaner - Temperature or Alarm Condition on CPXH Units
description: Troubleshooting CPXH temperature alarms, heating concerns, over-temperature behavior, or abnormal temperature display conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the ultrasonic cleaner from active use before troubleshooting. Do not handle hot solution, drain the tank, or move the unit until the bath temperature is safe. Expected outcome: No user is exposed to hot solution, electrical risk, or chemical splashing. Why it matters: CPXH units are heated ultrasonic cleaners, and temperature-related complaints may involve hot solution, steam, or chemical exposure.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm what clinical staff reported: Temperature alarm; Bath hotter than expected; Unit not heating; Temperature display abnormal; Heat indicator active but temperature not changing; Alarm appears only during certain cycles. Expected outcome: The exact symptom is confirmed before parts or repair are suspected.

steps.title: Check the Temperature Setpoint
steps.instructions: Review the programmed temperature setting on the CPXH control panel. CPXH models are heated units, with listed temperature capability up to about 69°C / 156°F depending on model and configuration. Confirm the setpoint is appropriate for the cleaning process and facility procedure. Expected outcome: The displayed setpoint matches the intended cleaning temperature. If the setpoint was accidentally set too high, correct it, allow the bath to stabilize, verify normal operation, and stop.

steps.title: Compare Displayed Temperature to Actual Bath Temperature
steps.instructions: Using a suitable external thermometer, verify the bath temperature away from the tank wall and away from items being cleaned. Compare the external reading to the CPXH display. Expected outcome: The displayed temperature is reasonably close to the measured solution temperature. Why it matters: CPXH temperature readout accuracy is listed as approximately ±3°C / ±5.4°F, so small differences may be normal.

steps.title: Confirm the Tank Is Filled Correctly
steps.instructions: Verify the solution level is at the proper operating level line. Do not operate the unit with low solution level. Add approved solution if needed. Expected outcome: The tank is filled to the proper level before heat or ultrasonics are tested. Why it matters: Low solution level can affect heating performance, temperature sensing, cavitation, and tank protection.

steps.title: Check for Incorrect Load Placement
steps.instructions: Inspect the tank for baskets, trays, instruments, beakers, or containers placed directly on the tank bottom. Items should be supported by an appropriate basket, tray, or accessory rather than resting directly on the tank floor. Expected outcome: The load is properly suspended and not interfering with heat transfer or ultrasonic operation. If correcting the load placement clears the alarm or abnormal behavior, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Solution Condition
steps.instructions: Check for heavily contaminated solution, excessive debris, incorrect chemistry, foaming, or chemical residue. Replace the solution if it is dirty, mixed incorrectly, or not appropriate for ultrasonic cleaning. Expected outcome: The bath contains clean, approved solution at the correct concentration. Why it matters: Solution condition can affect temperature response, cleaning performance, and sensor behavior.

steps.title: Verify Heat Operation
steps.instructions: With the tank properly filled and the unit safely positioned, activate heat and observe: Heat indicator behavior; Temperature rise over time; Whether the display changes gradually; Whether an alarm appears immediately or only after heating. Expected outcome: Temperature increases gradually and remains controlled near the selected setpoint. If the unit heats normally and no alarm returns, return to service after confirming safe operation.

steps.title: Check for Environmental Heat Sources
steps.instructions: Verify the unit is not located near: Sterilizers; Warming cabinets; Direct sunlight; HVAC discharge; Other equipment producing heat. Expected outcome: The cleaner is operating in a normal room environment without external heat influence. Why it matters: External heat can make the bath appear to overheat or delay cooling.

steps.title: Inspect Power Source and Cord Condition
steps.instructions: Verify the unit is connected to a properly grounded outlet. Inspect the power cord and plug for damage, loose fit, heat discoloration, or signs of arcing. Expected outcome: Power is stable and there is no visible electrical damage. If cord, plug, outlet, or heat damage is found, remove the unit from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Power Cycle and Retest
steps.instructions: Turn the unit off, allow it to cool if needed, then restart it. Re-enter the intended temperature setting and observe whether the alarm or temperature issue returns. Expected outcome: The unit restarts normally and maintains the selected temperature. If the alarm clears and does not return during a controlled test, document the test results and return the unit to service per facility policy.

steps.title: Check for Repeatability
steps.instructions: Run a short controlled test with: Correct solution level; No load or a normal approved basket; Correct setpoint; External temperature verification. Expected outcome: The temperature display, heater response, and alarm behavior remain normal. If the alarm repeats under controlled conditions, stop troubleshooting and escalate.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Branson Bransonic CPXH ultrasonic cleaner displayed a temperature alarm during heated operation.
CCR.Cause: Verified correct outlet and solution level, but alarm returned during controlled heat test with measured bath temperature above expected range.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, labeled Out of Service, documented temperature readings, and sent for bench evaluation of heater control and temperature sensing circuit.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual smell, heat, steam, or discoloration
Whether alarm occurred at startup or during heating

title: Branson Bransonic Series Ultrasonic Cleaner - Drain Clogging or Tank-Related Fluid Issue
description: Troubleshooting drain clogs, slow draining, leaking, standing fluid, and tank-related issues on Branson Bransonic ultrasonic cleaners.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Verify the ultrasonic cleaner is not in active use and that no instruments or contaminated items are being processed. Turn the unit off and unplug it before checking the tank, drain, hose, or exterior plumbing. Expected outcome: The unit is safe to inspect without electrical, splash, or contamination risk. Why it matters: Ultrasonic cleaners combine liquid, heat, and electrical components. Troubleshooting should not be performed while the unit is energized or clinically needed.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Fluid Issue
steps.instructions: Ask staff what was observed: Tank will not drain; Tank drains slowly; Fluid backs up into the tank; Fluid leaks near the drain; Fluid remains in the tank after draining; Tank appears stained, pitted, or contaminated; Drain valve is stiff, loose, or not closing fully. Expected outcome: The exact complaint is confirmed before troubleshooting begins.

steps.title: Check the Tank for Debris or Instrument Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the tank for loose items, instrument fragments, labels, packaging, gauze, plastic, broken glass, or heavy sediment. Remove any debris using appropriate PPE and facility cleaning precautions. Do not use sharp tools that could scratch or puncture the tank. Expected outcome: The tank is free of visible debris. If debris removal restores normal draining, clean the tank, test drain flow, document the finding, and stop.

steps.title: Verify Fluid Level and Solution Condition
steps.instructions: Check whether the tank is overfilled, filled with heavy residue, or loaded with solution that has excessive debris or buildup. Drain and refill according to facility procedure if the solution is heavily contaminated or appears thick, cloudy, or full of solids. Expected outcome: Fluid condition is suitable for normal draining and cleaning operation. Why it matters: Heavy debris or residue can settle near the drain opening and cause repeated clogging.

steps.title: Inspect the Drain Opening Inside the Tank
steps.instructions: Look at the drain outlet from inside the tank. Check for visible blockage, mineral buildup, instrument debris, tape, labels, or residue around the drain screen or opening. Clear only accessible debris. Do not force tools into the drain path. Expected outcome: The drain opening is visibly clear. If the drain begins flowing normally after clearing visible debris, run a controlled drain test and return the unit to service if no leaks are present.

steps.title: Check the External Drain Hose or Plumbing Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the drain hose, tubing, or connection point if the unit is connected to external drainage. Verify that the hose is: Not kinked; Not pinched behind the unit; Not routed uphill; Not submerged in standing fluid; Not clogged with debris; Securely attached to the drain fitting. Expected outcome: The external drain path allows fluid to flow freely. If correcting the hose route resolves the issue, test normal draining and stop.

steps.title: Check the Drain Valve Operation
steps.instructions: Open and close the drain valve through its normal range of motion. Check whether the valve feels stuck, loose, gritty, or misaligned. Confirm that staff are using the correct valve position for draining. Expected outcome: The valve opens fully, closes fully, and does not leak when closed. If the valve does not move correctly or leaks when closed, remove the unit from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Drain Test
steps.instructions: With the unit unplugged and positioned safely, add a small amount of clean water to the tank if appropriate. Open the drain and observe whether water drains smoothly without backing up, leaking, or pooling under the unit. Expected outcome: Water drains at a steady rate through the intended drain path. If the unit drains normally, dry the exterior, verify there are no leaks, and return it to service.

steps.title: Inspect for External Leakage
steps.instructions: Check around the drain fitting, valve area, underside, rear panel, and nearby countertop. Look for: Drips during draining; Fluid under the unit; Wet insulation or inaccessible internal areas; Corrosion around fittings; Cracks or deformation near the drain. Expected outcome: No fluid escapes outside the intended drain path. If leakage is present from the unit body, drain assembly, or underside, remove the unit from service.

steps.title: Inspect the Tank Surface
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the tank for cracks, dents, pitting, staining, corrosion, or signs of improper cleaning chemicals. Do not place the unit back into service if the tank appears compromised or if fluid may be entering internal areas. Expected outcome: Tank condition is acceptable for safe use. Why it matters: A compromised tank can leak into electrical components or create cleaning-performance and infection-control concerns.

steps.title: Verify Proper Basket and Accessory Use
steps.instructions: Confirm staff are using the correct basket or tray and are not placing instruments directly on the tank bottom. Expected outcome: Instruments are supported properly and not blocking the drain or damaging the tank. If improper loading caused the issue, educate staff, document the finding, and return the device to service after successful testing.

steps.title: Confirm Final Operation
steps.instructions: After corrections, verify: Tank fills normally; Drain opens and closes properly; Fluid drains completely; No leaks are present; Tank is clean and free of obstruction; Unit powers on normally after exterior is dry. Expected outcome: The Bransonic cleaner is safe to return to service.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Branson Bransonic ultrasonic cleaner would not drain completely after instrument cleaning cycles.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found debris buildup at the tank drain opening and a partially kinked external drain hose.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared accessible debris, corrected drain hose routing, performed a controlled drain test, and verified normal draining with no external leakage.

helpfulDetails: Whether the unit was unplugged before inspection
Whether hose was kinked or routed uphill
Tank condition: stains, pitting, cracks, corrosion, dents
Whether accessories or baskets were being used correctly
Power behavior after the exterior was dry
Alarm behavior or indicator lights, if present
